questions.question: Logic (declarative) programming is described as non-procedural. What does this mean?
options: Logic programs cannot call subprograms
Logic programs describe WHAT the result looks like, not HOW to compute it step by step
Logic programs execute only one statement at a time
Logic programs do not use variables
questions.answer: Logic programs describe WHAT the result looks like, not HOW to compute it step by step
questions.explanation: Declarative/logic programming specifies the FORM of the result (what must be true of the answer) using predicate calculus, not the algorithm to produce it. Prolog's inferencing engine finds a proof. This contrasts with imperative programming where the programmer specifies every step. For example: declaring 'a list is sorted if it is a permutation and its elements are ordered' rather than implementing bubble sort.

questions.question: In Prolog, how are constants distinguished from variables syntactically?
options: Constants are enclosed in quotes; variables are not
Constants start with a lowercase letter or are numeric; variables start with an uppercase letter
Constants are integers; variables are atoms
There is no syntactic difference — context determines which is which
questions.answer: Constants start with a lowercase letter or are numeric; variables start with an uppercase letter
questions.explanation: Prolog follows a strict naming convention: atoms (constants) start with a lowercase letter (e.g., `joe`, `apple`) or are numeric (e.g., `42`). Variables start with an uppercase letter (e.g., `X`, `MyVar`). This distinction is crucial — lowercase `x` is an atom (constant object), uppercase `X` is a variable that can be instantiated.

questions.question: Given the Prolog fact `father(bill, jake).`, what does this represent?
options: A rule that bill is the father of jake if certain conditions are met
A query asking if bill is the father of jake
A headless Horn clause — a fact that bill is the father of jake
A headed Horn clause — a conditional relationship
questions.answer: A headless Horn clause — a fact that bill is the father of jake
questions.explanation: A Prolog fact is a headless Horn clause — it has no left-hand side (no consequent). It unconditionally asserts a proposition as true. `father(bill, jake).` states that bill is the father of jake without any conditions. A headed Horn clause (rule) has the form `head :- body.` with conditions on the right side.

questions.question: Consider the Prolog rule: `grandparent(X, Z) :- parent(X, Y), parent(Y, Z).` How should this be read?
options: X is the grandparent of Z if X is the parent of Y or Y is the parent of Z
If X is the grandparent of Z, then X is the parent of Y and Y is the parent of Z
X is the grandparent of Z if X is the parent of Y and Y is the parent of Z
X is the grandparent of Y and Y is the parent of Z
questions.answer: X is the grandparent of Z if X is the parent of Y and Y is the parent of Z
questions.explanation: The `:-` operator means 'if' — the head (left side) is true IF the body (right side) is true. Commas in the body represent logical AND. So `grandparent(X, Z) :- parent(X, Y), parent(Y, Z)` reads: 'X is the grandparent of Z IF X is the parent of Y AND Y is the parent of Z.' The body is the antecedent; the head is the consequent.

questions.question: Prolog uses backward chaining (top-down resolution) as its inferencing strategy. Starting from `?- man(bob).` with rules `man(X) :- father(X).` and fact `father(bob).`, which approach describes backward chaining?
options: Start with the fact `father(bob)`, apply the rule, conclude `man(bob)`
Start with the goal `man(bob)`, find a rule `man(X) :- father(X)`, unify X=bob, then verify `father(bob)` is a fact
Generate all possible facts and check if any lead to `man(bob)`
Start from the middle of the database and work both directions
questions.answer: Start with the goal `man(bob)`, find a rule `man(X) :- father(X)`, unify X=bob, then verify `father(bob)` is a fact
questions.explanation: Backward chaining starts with the GOAL and works backward to facts. Goal: prove `man(bob)`. Find rule matching `man(X)` — unify X=bob, yielding subgoal `father(bob)`. Now prove `father(bob)` — found as a fact. Chain complete: `man(bob)` is proven. Forward chaining (bottom-up) starts from facts. Prolog uses backward chaining with depth-first search.

questions.question: Prolog uses depth-first search when proving goals with multiple subgoals. What does this mean?
options: All subgoals are proved simultaneously in parallel
A complete proof is found for the first subgoal before moving to the second, and so on
Subgoals are sorted by difficulty before proving
The most recently added fact is tried first
questions.answer: A complete proof is found for the first subgoal before moving to the second, and so on
questions.explanation: Depth-first search: Prolog fully attempts to prove the leftmost subgoal first. If it succeeds, it moves to the next subgoal. If any subgoal fails, Prolog backtracks to the previous subgoal and tries a different instantiation. Breadth-first would work on all subgoals in parallel — less efficient. Prolog's depth-first approach uses fewer computational resources.

questions.question: Consider the trace for `?- likes(jake, X), likes(darcie, X).` — X is first instantiated to `chocolate`, then `likes(darcie, chocolate)` fails. What happens next?
options: Prolog reports `false` immediately
Prolog backtracks to the first subgoal and tries the next matching fact for `likes(jake, X)`
Prolog tries to prove `likes(darcie, X)` with a different X
Prolog swaps the order of the subgoals
questions.answer: Prolog backtracks to the first subgoal and tries the next matching fact for `likes(jake, X)`
questions.explanation: When the second subgoal fails, Prolog BACKTRACKS — it returns to the most recent choice point (the first subgoal) and tries the NEXT fact. `likes(jake, chocolate)` was tried first. After failure on `likes(darcie, chocolate)`, Prolog backtracks and tries `likes(jake, apricots)`. This gives X=apricots. Then `likes(darcie, apricots)` succeeds.

questions.question: The Prolog `is` operator is used for arithmetic. Which statement correctly describes its usage?
options: `A is B + C` assigns the value of B+C to A; A can be re-assigned later
`A is B + C` instantiates A (must be uninstantiated) to the value of B+C (B and C must be instantiated)
`A is B + C` is a fact stating that A always equals B+C
`A is B + C` compares A to the value of B+C and returns true or false
questions.answer: `A is B + C` instantiates A (must be uninstantiated) to the value of B+C (B and C must be instantiated)
questions.explanation: The `is` operator evaluates its right-hand side arithmetically and instantiates the left-hand variable. Rules: (1) All variables on the RHS must be already instantiated; (2) The LHS variable must be UNINSTANTIATED. `Sum is Sum + Number` is always illegal — if Sum is instantiated, the LHS is instantiated (illegal); if not, the RHS uses it uninstantiated (illegal). Prolog variables are not mutable like imperative variables.

questions.question: In Prolog list syntax, `[Head | Tail]` is used in rules. What does `Tail` represent?
options: The last element of the list
All elements of the list except the first (the rest of the list)
The head of the list, which is identical to Head
A single element that comes after Head
questions.answer: All elements of the list except the first (the rest of the list)
questions.explanation: `[Head | Tail]` pattern-matches a non-empty list: `Head` is the FIRST element, and `Tail` is a list containing ALL REMAINING elements. For `[a, b, c]`: Head=a, Tail=[b, c]. For `[a]`: Head=a, Tail=[]. The `|` operator separates the head from the tail. This is equivalent to Scheme's `car`/`cdr` decomposition.

questions.question:
The Prolog `member` proposition is:
```
member(Element, [Element | _]).
member(Element, [_ | Lis]) :- member(Element, Lis).
```
What does the underscore `_` represent?
options: A variable that must be proved to be false
An anonymous variable — a placeholder for a value we don't care about
An empty list
A special constant representing any atom
questions.answer: An anonymous variable — a placeholder for a value we don't care about
questions.explanation: The underscore `_` is an anonymous variable — it matches anything but is never bound to a name. In `member(Element, [Element | _])`, we don't care what the tail of the list is — we just need to check if Element matches the head. In `[_ | Lis]`, we don't care what the head is — we want the tail `Lis`. Each `_` is a separate anonymous variable.

questions.question: Why must the base case `member(Element, [Element | _]).` appear BEFORE the recursive case in the `member` proposition?
options: Prolog requires facts before rules alphabetically
Prolog matches from top to bottom — if the recursive case were first, it would recurse infinitely without ever matching the head
The base case is faster, so placing it first optimises performance
Prolog's compiler requires facts before recursive rules for type checking
questions.answer: Prolog matches from top to bottom — if the recursive case were first, it would recurse infinitely without ever matching the head
questions.explanation: Prolog tries facts and rules from TOP to BOTTOM. If the recursive case came first, every call would immediately recursively strip the head and search the tail — the base case (head matches) would never be reached, causing infinite recursion. Placing the base case first ensures: (1) if the element IS the head, it matches and succeeds immediately; (2) only if it doesn't match the head do we recurse.

questions.question: Given facts `parent(bill, jake)` and `parent(bill, shelley)`, and rule `sibling(X, Y) :- parent(M, X), parent(M, Y).`, what does `?- sibling(X, Y).` return first?
options: X = jake, Y = shelley
X = jake, Y = jake
false — no siblings found
X = bill, Y = bill
questions.answer: X = jake, Y = jake
questions.explanation: Prolog instantiates M=bill (from `parent(bill, jake)`), X=jake. Then for `parent(M, Y)` with M=bill, the first match is `parent(bill, jake)` giving Y=jake. So X=jake, Y=jake is returned — a person is found to be their own sibling! To fix this, add `not(X=Y)` to the rule. This illustrates Prolog's negation problem.

questions.question: The closed-world assumption in Prolog means that if `?- man(fred).` returns `false`, what can we conclude?
options: Fred is definitely not a man
Prolog cannot prove that Fred is a man given the current facts and rules, but Fred might still be a man
There is an error in the Prolog database
Fred is a woman
questions.answer: Prolog cannot prove that Fred is a man given the current facts and rules, but Fred might still be a man
questions.explanation: The closed-world assumption: Prolog treats anything it CANNOT PROVE as false. If `man(fred).` is not in the database and no rule can derive it, Prolog answers `false`. This does NOT logically mean Fred is not a man — it means the information is missing or incomplete. The world is 'closed' to only what is in the database.

questions.question: The Prolog `not` operator is not a true logical negation. `not(X=Y)` succeeds when:
options: X and Y are definitely different objects
Prolog cannot prove that X equals Y — meaning the equality cannot be derived from current facts
X and Y have been bound to different values
The unification of X and Y would create an infinite loop
questions.answer: Prolog cannot prove that X equals Y — meaning the equality cannot be derived from current facts
questions.explanation: Prolog's `not(Goal)` succeeds if `Goal` FAILS (cannot be proved), and fails if `Goal` succeeds. `not(X=Y)` does not mean 'X is logically different from Y' — it means 'Prolog cannot prove X=Y'. This is a consequence of the closed-world assumption applied to negation. It works practically for simple cases but is philosophically not a true logical NOT.

questions.question: The ordering of subgoals in a Prolog query affects efficiency. Why is `?- parent(X, shelley), male(X).` more efficient than `?- male(X), parent(X, shelley).`?
options: The `parent` predicate is always faster than `male`
`parent(X, shelley)` constrains X to at most 2 values (shelley's parents) before testing maleness, while `male(X)` may iterate over many males before finding shelley's parent
Prolog executes the second subgoal first in both versions
Using `parent` first reduces the number of facts Prolog needs to load
questions.answer: `parent(X, shelley)` constrains X to at most 2 values (shelley's parents) before testing maleness, while `male(X)` may iterate over many males before finding shelley's parent
questions.explanation: Prolog works left-to-right on subgoals. `male(X)` first: tries every male in the database — if there are 1000 males and none are shelley's parent, all 1000 are checked. `parent(X, shelley)` first: at most 2 values of X (shelley's parents) are tried before testing maleness. Ordering subgoals to be most selective first dramatically reduces search. This is a deficiency — order should not matter in pure logic.

questions.question:
The Prolog `append` proposition:
```
append([], Lis, Lis).
append([Head | Lis_1], Lis_2, [Head | Lis_3]) :- append(Lis_1, Lis_2, Lis_3).
```
What does `?- append([a, b], X, [a, b, c, d]).` compute?
options: X = [a, b, c, d]
X = [c, d]
X = [a, b]
false — X is on the wrong side
questions.answer: X = [c, d]
questions.explanation: This query asks: 'What list X, when appended to [a, b], gives [a, b, c, d]?' Prolog unifies and works through the recursive rule: strip `a` from first list, head of result. Strip `b`, head of result. First list is empty — base case fires: `Lis = X` must equal `[c, d]` (the remaining result). So `X = [c, d]`. The append proposition can work BACKWARDS — a key feature of logic programming.

questions.question: In Prolog list processing, what is the key difference compared to Scheme list processing?
options: Prolog uses recursive strategies but results are expressed as parameters, not return values; false cases are not explicitly stated
Prolog uses iterative loops while Scheme uses recursion
Prolog processes lists left to right; Scheme processes them right to left
Prolog only works with lists of atoms; Scheme works with any list
questions.answer: Prolog uses recursive strategies but results are expressed as parameters, not return values; false cases are not explicitly stated
questions.explanation: Both Scheme and Prolog use recursive strategies for list processing. Key Prolog differences: (1) No return values — results are expressed as additional parameters (e.g., `append(X, Y, Z)` where Z is the result); (2) No explicit false cases — if no matching clause succeeds, the proposition is automatically false; (3) Separate clauses for base and recursive cases instead of `cond`.

questions.question: The Prolog tracing model has four events. Which event occurs when Prolog successfully proves a subgoal?
options: Call
Exit
Redo
Fail
questions.answer: Exit
questions.explanation: The four tracing events are: (1) **Call** — beginning of an attempt to satisfy a goal; (2) **Exit** — a goal has been successfully satisfied/proved; (3) **Redo** — backtracking occurs, and Prolog tries to re-satisfy a goal with a different instantiation; (4) **Fail** — a goal cannot be proved. In the trace, `(1) Exit: likes(jake, chocolate)` means that goal was satisfied.

questions.question: Prolog's intrinsic limitations mean some programs are highly inefficient. The sort-by-permutation program repeatedly generates permutations of a list and tests each one. What is the worst-case time complexity of this approach for a list of n elements?
options: O(n log n) — same as comparison-based sorting
O(n²) — quadratic
O(n!) — factorial, since there are n! permutations to potentially check
O(2^n) — exponential
questions.answer: O(n!) — factorial, since there are n! permutations to potentially check
questions.explanation: The permutation-based sort generates ALL permutations of the list and tests each for sortedness. A list of n elements has n! permutations. In the worst case (the sorted permutation is last), all n! permutations are checked. For n=10, that's 3,628,800 checks. An efficient sort (like mergesort) is O(n log n). This illustrates why Prolog programs can be extremely inefficient for some tasks.

questions.question: The `reverse` Prolog proposition uses `append` recursively. Given `?- reverse([a, b, c], X).`, which answer is correct?
options: X = [a, b, c]
X = [c, b, a]
X = [b, a, c]
X = [c, a, b]
questions.answer: X = [c, b, a]
questions.explanation: The reverse proposition strategy: 'The reverse of [Head | Tail] is the reverse of Tail, with Head appended at the end.' Working through [a, b, c]: reverse([b, c]) first = [c, b]; then append [c, b] with [a] to get [c, b, a]. The base case `reverse([], [])` terminates the recursion. So `reverse([a, b, c], X)` gives X = [c, b, a].
